coredata.dc:title: Development of a long-term fluorescent invisible marker using blue-emitting carbon dots
coredata.dc:creator: Gopi, Pavithra
Ponnusamy, Kathirvel
Kandhasamy, Karthick
coredata.dc:description: The development of novel anti-counterfeiting materials and technologies is vital in order to combat counterfeit activities, as existing technologies such as optical watermarking, micro-printing, laser holography, barcodes, RFID, and QR codes are widely known to counterfeiters and easily replicated. In particular, several challenges and issues, including high costs, low yields during synthesis, and the photo-bleaching effect associated with the use of luminescent materials in anti-counterfeiting applications are still need to be addressed. Carbon Dots (CDs) is a new emergent in carbon family with excellent luminescence properties. In this report, we describe a fast, cost-effective, and simple one-step heating method for synthesizing CDs. An invisible marker was produced by incorporating blue emitting CDs into the reservoir of a commercial sketch pen. The writings using the marker were not visible under normal light but emitted blue fluorescence under UV light (365 nm). The marker retained its fluorescence for up to six months without any decrease visible to the naked eye.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Invisible ink
Anti-counterfeiting
UV active ink

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We acknowledge G. Manikandan for his support in conducting FTIR facility, S. Sruti Nanditaa for her invaluable assistance with the UV-Vis spectroscopy at PSG College of Technology and PSG Institute of Advanced Studies for providing support in using characterization techniques.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Preparation of CDs on hotplate, (b) Filtration using dialysis membrane (c) prepared CDs solution under visible and UV light (365 nm) and (d) making invisible marker by incorporating CDs into reservoir of sketch pen.
(a) HRTEM image of CDs with SAEDP inlet, (b) Size distribution graph, (c) XRD, (d) Raman, (e) FTIR (f) UV Visible and PL spectrum of CDs and (g) Schematic of energy transition of CDs.

(a) Developed invisible marker, (b) Red shift in PL spectrum of CDs in solution form after six months (inset: Quenched CDs solution), Comparison of marker writings under visible and UV light (c) after development and (d) after six months, (e, f) Writing using CDs on thermocol and cloth dipped in CDs under visible and UV light.

head.ce:title: Development of a long-term fluorescent invisible marker using blue-emitting carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: GRD Centre for Materials Research, Department of Physics, PSG College of Technology, Coimbatore 641004, Tamil Nadu, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: GRD Centre for Materials Research, Department of Physics, PSG College of Technology, Coimbatore 641004, Tamil Nadu, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The development of novel anti-counterfeiting materials and technologies is vital in order to combat counterfeit activities, as existing technologies such as optical watermarking, micro-printing, laser holography, barcodes, RFID, and QR codes are widely known to counterfeiters and easily replicated. In particular, several challenges and issues, including high costs, low yields during synthesis, and the photo-bleaching effect associated with the use of luminescent materials in anti-counterfeiting applications are still need to be addressed. Carbon Dots (CDs) is a new emergent in carbon family with excellent luminescence properties. In this report, we describe a fast, cost-effective, and simple one-step heating method for synthesizing CDs. An invisible marker was produced by incorporating blue emitting CDs into the reservoir of a commercial sketch pen. The writings using the marker were not visible under normal light but emitted blue fluorescence under UV light (365 nm). The marker retained its fluorescence for up to six months without any decrease visible to the naked eye.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Counterfeit products have become a significant issue that has captured the attention of governments, economists, and business leaders [1]. As a result, there has been an increased demand for the development of anti-counterfeiting technology. One of the most promising techniques for combating counterfeit products is printing luminescent material with high resolution and improved anti-counterfeiting performance [2].

Carbon dots have gained tremendous attraction in recent years due to their luminescent properties. CDs are quasi-spherical structure particles that possess oxygen/nitrogen based groups attached to hybrid sp2/sp3 core. CDs are cost-effective, non-toxic, biocompatible, soluble, and exhibit tunable luminescence and stability. The luminescence of CDs depends upon their size, defects and functional groups on their surface [3]. High solution dispersion of CDs with strong luminescence can be used as invisible ink in anti-counterfeiting field. While extensive research has explored the utilization of CDs as fluorescent ink, a notable challenge lies in fluorescence quenching over prolonged storage periods, particularly when employing surface passivation with polymers [4]. This study addresses the challenge by aiming to reduce aggregation without resorting to composite formation. CDs were prepared through a simple and cost-effective one-step heating method, using citric acid and glycerol as the carbon source and diethylenetriamine as the nitrogen source. The resulting CDs appeared transparent to the naked eye under visible light and exhibited vibrant blue fluorescence under UV light. The stability and durability of the CDs, utilized as invisible ink for anti-counterfeiting applications, were examined over long-term storage by injecting them into the reservoir of a sketch pen.

The initial solution was prepared by adding 10 g of citric acid, 10 ml glycerol and 5 ml diethylenetriamine into 10 ml to distilled water and stirred with magnetic stirring for 15 min. After complete dissolution, clear solution was obtained. The solution was then heated on hotplate which was preheated to 200 ˚C. Brownish yellow solution was obtained after 10 min. The solution was subjected to filtration using dialysis membrane for 24 h.

Commercial sketch pen was bought from a stationary shop. The dye in the felt tip and reservoir of the sketch was removed by soaking them in acetone for three days. The acetone was replaced with fresh acetone every 12 h. The filtered CDs was injected into the felt tip and reservoir using syringe, as shown in Fig. 1 .

The structure of CDs was examined using High-Resolution Transmission Electron Microscopy (HRTEM). In Fig. 2 a, the dispersed image of CDs is depicted. The histogram plot in Fig. 2b displays a size distribution ranging from 1.8 to 2.8 nm, with an average size of 2.2 nm. The absence of rings and spots in the Selected Area Electron Diffraction pattern is indicative of the disordered structure of CDs. This disordered arrangement is further affirmed through X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) and Raman spectroscopy in Fig. 2c and 2d. The broad XRD peaks at 20.6° (002) and 43.5˚ (100) affirm the amorphous nature of CDs [5]. The Raman peaks at 1342 cm−1 (D band) and 1588 cm−1 (G band) denote the presence of sp3 hybridized defects and sp2bonded carbon atoms. Additionally, the ID/IG ratio of 0.9 signifies the presence of a disordered sp2 hybridized graphitic structure of CDs [6]. The surface bonds formed due to aromatization and condensation of organic precursors are determined by Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) spectrum in Fig. 2e. The peak at 2854 and 2925 cm−1 corresponds to symmetric stretching vibration of C-H bond [7]. Peak at 1730 cm−1 indicates the presence of stretching vibration of C=O amide bond [8]. The peaks at 1570 cm−1 and 1393 cm−1 can be attributed to the stretching vibration of C=N and C-N bonds, respectively, indicating the presence of nitrogen in CDs [7]. The existence of C-O and C-OH bonds are obtained by the stretching vibration of C-O at 1114 cm−1 [9]. The symmetric stretching vibration of C-O-C is observed at 1045 cm−1. Presence of functional groups improves the solubility of CDs in aqueous medium, facilitating the production of ink [10].

Fig. 2f shows the absorption and emission spectra of CD measured using UV–Vis and Photoluminescence Spectrophotometer. CDs display absorbance peaks at 230 nm and 350 nm, corresponding to π-π* transition of aromatic C=C bonds and n-π* transition of the C=O or C=N bonds, respectively [9,11]. The emission spectrum of CDs were measured with excitation at 350 nm. CDs showed blue fluorescence emission at 453 nm (Fig. 2g). The emission is assigned to the size and surface state produced due to nitrogen in CDs [3,12].

This blue emissive CDs was used as ink in a sketch pen (Fig. 3 a). Fig. 3 (c-f) shows the pictures of writings under visible and UV light (365 nm). The words that were written on the paper using CDs ink were not discernible to the naked eye, but they exhibited blue fluorescence when viewed under ultraviolet light. A white cloth was dipped into CDs solution and viewed under UV light. Even after multiple washes using water, the cloth retained its blue emission (Fig. 3f). This determines that the CDs have excellent adherence to paper and cloth. Hence, CDs can be utilized as UV-active ink.

One notable shortcoming of solution processed CDs is aggregation. Yi Ru et al. [13] posits that planar aromatic organic molecules can undergo fluorescence quenching due to strong intermolecular π-π stacking caused by their large polycyclic aromatic structure. This results in a nonradioactive transition from photo excited state to ground state. Thus, superimposition of π – π stacking in CDs induces self-aggregation, influencing luminescent quenching. The aggregation of CDs can be controlled by various methods like surface passivation, heteroatom doping or embedding in solid matrices like polymers [14,15]. The felt tip and reservoir of the sketch pen are made of synthetic polyester fiber. When CDs are inserted into the reservoir, the aggregation of CDs reduces due to void space in synthetic fiber. This increases the stability of CDs by suppressing the aggregation induced emission nature of CDs, thereby increasing durability of the product. Fig. 3b illustrates the durability by depicting a red shift in the PL spectrum of liquid CDs, resulting in a greenish emission under UV light after being stored for six months. However, from Fig. 3d it is evident that the invisible ink retains blue emission even after six months of storage.

In conclusion, the one-step heating method used in the preparation of CDs proved to be a faster, simpler and cost-effective approach compared to other bottom-up techniques. The CDs exhibited a remarkable bright blue fluorescence when exposed to UV light, making them an excellent candidate for UV active ink. To evaluate the anti-counterfeiting potential of CDs, they were utilized as ink to create an invisible marker that appeared blue under UV light but was invisible under visible light. Furthermore, the adherence of CDs to the fibers of the reservoir improved the durability of the marker by reducing the aggregation of CDs. Overall, our study demonstrates a simple concept for preparing highly durable invisible markers, with promising applications in anti-counterfeiting technology.

Pavithra Gopi: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Investigation, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Kathirvel Ponnusamy: Validation, Supervision. Karthick Kandhasamy: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Investigation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We acknowledge G. Manikandan for his support in conducting FTIR facility, S. Sruti Nanditaa for her invaluable assistance with the UV-Vis spectroscopy at PSG College of Technology and PSG Institute of Advanced Studies for providing support in using characterization techniques.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Luminescence anti-counterfeiting: from elementary to advanced

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Yu, H. Zhang, J. Yu, Luminescence anti‐counterfeiting: From elementary to advanced, Aggregate. 2 (2021) 20–34. https://doi.org/10.1002/agt2.15.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Anticounterfeiting technology – a luminescent path: short review

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Helen Ratna Monica, Jyoti, Pranav Y., Anticounterfeiting Technology – A Luminescent Path: Short Review, J. Adv. Chem. Sci. 7 (2021) 706–710. https://doi.org/10.30799/jacs.233.21070103.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon quantum dots: an emerging material for optoelectronic applications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: T. Yuan, T. Meng, P. He, Y. Shi, Y. Li, X. Li, L. Fan, S. Yang, Carbon quantum dots: An emerging material for optoelectronic applications, J. Mater. Chem. C. 7 (2019) 6820–6835. https://doi.org/10.1039/c9tc01730e.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon dots as emerging luminophores in security inks for anti-counterfeit applications - An up-to-date review

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: K. Muthamma, D. Sunil, P. Shetty, Carbon dots as emerging luminophores in security inks for anti-counterfeit applications - An up-to-date review, Appl. Mater. Today. 23 (2021) 101050. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apmt.2021.101050.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dot/graphene hybrid nanocomposite as an efficient catalyst support for the oxygen reduction reaction

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Cao, Y. Hu, L. Chen, J. Xu, Z. Chen, Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dot/graphene hybrid nanocomposite as an efficient catalyst support for the oxygen reduction reaction, Int. J. Hydrogen Energy. 42 (2017) 2931–2942. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijhydene.2017.01.029.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Enhancing photoluminescence stability of blue emissive nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots by encapsulating inside zeolitic imidazole framework-8

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: T. Balakrishnan, W.L. Ang, E. Mahmoudi, Enhancing photoluminescence stability of blue emissive nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots by encapsulating inside zeolitic imidazole framework-8, J. Solid State Chem. 328 (2023) 124343. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jssc.2023.124343.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Anti-counterfeiting application of fluorescent carbon dots derived from wasted coffee grounds

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: W.T. Hong, H.K. Yang, Anti-counterfeiting application of fluorescent carbon dots derived from wasted coffee grounds, Optik (Stuttg). 241 (2021) 166449. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijleo.2021.166449.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The design of room-temperature-phosphorescent carbon dots and their application as a security ink

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Li, S. Ye, J.Q. Guo, J.T. Kong, J. Song, Z.H. Kang, J. Le Qu, The design of room-temperature-phosphorescent carbon dots and their application as a security ink, J. Mater. Chem. C. 7 (2019) 10605–10612. https://doi.org/10.1039/c9tc03481a.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Highly fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon dots with excellent thermal and photo stability applied as invisible ink for loading important information and anti-counterfeiting

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Liu, L. Zhou, Y. Li, R. Deng, H. Zhang, Highly fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon dots with excellent thermal and photo stability applied as invisible ink for loading important information and anti-counterfeiting, Nanoscale. 9 (2017) 491–496. https://doi.org/10.1039/c6nr07123f.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Hydrothermal conversion of Magnolia liliiflora into nitrogen-doped carbon dots as an effective turn-off fluorescence sensing, multi-colour cell imaging and fluorescent ink

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R. Atchudan, T.N.J.I. Edison, K.R. Aseer, S. Perumal, Y.R. Lee, Hydrothermal conversion of Magnolia liliiflora into nitrogen-doped carbon dots as an effective turn-off fluorescence sensing, multi-colour cell imaging and fluorescent ink, Colloids Surfaces B Biointerfaces. 169 (2018) 321–328. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.colsurfb.2018.05.032.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Induction coil heater prepared highly fluorescent carbon dots as invisible ink and explosive sensor

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M.P. Sk, A. Chattopadhyay, Induction coil heater prepared highly fluorescent carbon dots as invisible ink and explosive sensor, RSC Adv. 4 (2014) 31994–31999. https://doi.org/10.1039/c4ra04264f.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Zhu, Y. Song, X. Zhao, J. Shao, J. Zhang, B. Yang, The photoluminescence mechanism in carbon dots (graphene quantum dots, carbon nanodots, and polymer dots): current state and future perspective, Nano Res. 8 (2015) 355–381. https://doi.org/10.1007/s12274-014-0644-3.

Pigment. 187 (2021). https://doi.org/10.1016/j.dyepig.2020.109090.
